title: Does turning the thermostat up heat a room faster?
description: No. On most UK heating systems the thermostat is an on/off switch, so 25 heats no faster than 20. Here is why, what overshooting costs, and what genuinely speeds heating up.
blurb: Why cranking the dial to 25 warms a cold room no faster, and the fixes that genuinely do.
lede: You walk in from the cold, the house feels like a fridge, and your hand goes straight to the thermostat. Twenty is not going to cut it, so you spin it to 25 to get things moving. It is one of the most natural instincts in home heating, and on almost every UK system it does absolutely nothing to speed the room up. It is the heating equivalent of pressing the lift button five times. This guide explains what your thermostat actually controls, what cranking it costs you when you forget to turn it back down, the handful of systems where the setting genuinely does change behaviour, and the things that really do warm a room faster.
short_answer: No. A standard room thermostat is an on/off switch, not a throttle. Your boiler delivers heat at the same rate whether the dial says 20 or 25, so the room reaches 20 degrees at exactly the same moment either way. All a higher setting does is keep the boiler running past the point you wanted, and every degree of overshoot adds roughly 10 per cent to the heat that room of the house is using, which is around £70 a year per degree on a typical gas-heated home at current prices.

sections.h2: How a room thermostat actually works
sections.html:
Most room thermostats, from a 1980s dial on the hallway wall to a basic digital model, are simple switches. They compare the air temperature around them with the number you have set. If the room is colder than the setting, the switch closes and tells the boiler to fire. If the room is at or above the setting, the switch opens and the boiler stops. That is the entire job. Engineers call it on/off or bang-bang control.

Crucially, the thermostat has no way of telling the boiler to work harder. The boiler heats water and pumps it round the radiators at whatever rate it heats water, set by its output and its flow temperature, not by the number on the wall. Setting 25 instead of 20 does not open the gas valve wider, spin the pump faster, or make the radiators hotter. The room warms along exactly the same curve in both cases. The only difference is where the boiler eventually switches off.

The lift-button comparison really is exact. Pressing the button repeatedly does not summon the lift faster, because the button is a request, not a throttle. Your thermostat setting is the same: a target, not a speed.

This sits alongside a family of similar misunderstandings we cover across our heating guides, including the closely related question of whether it is cheaper to leave the heating on all day. In both cases the physics gives a clear answer that disagrees with the folk wisdom.

sections.h2: What cranking it to 25 actually costs
sections.html:
If turning the dial up did nothing at all, this would be a harmless habit. The problem is what happens after the room reaches the temperature you actually wanted. With the thermostat at 25, the boiler sails straight past 20 and keeps going. Heat loss from a house rises with the temperature difference between inside and outside, so every extra degree you hold the house at costs real money for as long as you hold it there.

The standard rule of thumb, used by the Energy Saving Trust among others, is that each degree on the thermostat changes your space-heating energy use by roughly 10 per cent over a heating season. Here is what that looks like for a typical three-bed semi using about 10,000 kWh of gas a year for space heating:

Thermostat setting held all seasonApprox. gas for heatingApprox. annual costVersus 20 degrees
18 degrees8,000 kWh£586£147 less
19 degrees9,000 kWh£660£73 less
20 degrees10,000 kWh£733baseline
21 degrees11,000 kWh£806£73 more
22 degrees12,000 kWh£880£147 more
25 degrees (dial left where you cranked it)15,000 kWh£1,100£367 more

At 7.33p per kWh for gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Assumes roughly 10,000 kWh a year of gas for space heating at a 20 degree setting and a change of about 10 per cent per degree. Your home will differ; the proportions hold.

Nobody holds 25 degrees all winter on purpose. The real cost comes from forgetting. Crank the dial at 6pm, get comfortable by 7, and the heating quietly runs three or four degrees past your comfort point all evening. Do that most cold evenings and you are paying a meaningful slice of that bottom row for warmth you never asked for and probably vented out of a window when the room got stuffy.

sections.h2: The exceptions: systems where the setting does change behaviour
sections.html:
There are a few setups where the thermostat setting genuinely influences more than the switch-off point, and it is worth knowing if you have one.

Modulating controls (OpenTherm and load compensation)

Some modern combi boilers paired with a compatible smart thermostat talk to each other over a protocol such as OpenTherm. Instead of a bare on/off signal, the thermostat tells the boiler how far the room is from target, and the boiler adjusts its flow temperature to suit. With one of these, a big gap between actual and target can make the boiler run hotter water through the radiators for a while, so the room does warm slightly faster at a higher setting. The catch is that hotter flow water pushes a condensing boiler out of its most efficient range, so you trade efficiency for speed. The sensible move is still to set the temperature you want and let the control loop do its job.

TPI thermostats

Many digital programmable thermostats use time proportional and integral control. They pulse the boiler on and off in short bursts as the room approaches target, which reduces overshoot. The setting still does not change heating speed from cold; it just makes the landing softer.

Heat pumps: do not crank, ever

If you have an air source heat pump, turning the thermostat right up is actively expensive rather than merely pointless. Heat pumps are designed to run long and low, and many systems respond to a large jump in demand by switching on a backup immersion or resistance heater. That backup heats at roughly one third of the heat pump's efficiency, so on electricity at 26.11p per kWh you can briefly pay three to four times your normal price per unit of heat. Small setpoint changes and patience are the rule. Our guide to how heat pumps work covers why slow and steady is the entire design philosophy.

sections.h2: What actually heats a room faster
sections.html:
If your real complaint is that rooms take too long to warm up, there are levers that genuinely work, and none of them is the thermostat dial.

  • Raise the boiler flow temperature for cold snaps. This is the real throttle on a gas system. A boiler set to 60 degrees flow heats radiators noticeably faster than one set to 50. Most of the year a low flow temperature saves money through better condensing, but if the house genuinely cannot get warm on the coldest days, a temporary bump is the legitimate fix. Our flow temperature guide explains the trade-off and the settings to use.
  • Bleed and balance the radiators. A radiator that is cold at the top has air in it; one that is cool while others roar has a balancing problem. Both slow the room down every single day.
  • Open the TRV fully in the room you want fast. Thermostatic radiator valves throttle flow to individual radiators. A TRV on 2 or 3 will choke the very radiator you are standing next to.
  • Uncover the radiator. A sofa pushed against it or curtains draped over it can swallow a large share of its output. Radiators heat mostly by convection and need air moving across them.
  • Shut the door and close the curtains. A small sealed room warms several times faster than one feeding its heat to the hallway and out through cold glazing.

If a room stays stubbornly cold even with the heating running hard, something else is wrong, often sludge in the radiator, an undersized emitter, or serious draughts. Our guide to why a house feels cold with the heating on walks through the diagnosis in order.

The honest answer to "how do I make the room warm when I get home" is not a higher number, it is an earlier start. A basic programmer set to fire the heating 30 to 45 minutes before you arrive achieves what no amount of dial-spinning can.

Smart thermostats automate this with a feature called optimum start. The thermostat learns how quickly your home warms in different weather and starts the boiler at whatever time is needed so the house hits your target at the time you asked for, rather than starting heating at that time. On a cold morning it might fire 90 minutes early; on a mild one, 20. It is the correct engineering answer to the exact frustration that makes people crank the dial.

Whatever controls you have, the target itself matters more than anything else on this page. Most households are comfortable somewhere between 18 and 21 degrees, and our guide to what temperature to set your thermostat covers how to find your number, room by room. The broader thermostat settings hub collects everything on schedules, setbacks, and controls in one place.

On a standard UK heating system, the thermostat is a target, not an accelerator. The room hits 20 degrees at the same moment whether the dial says 20 or 25, and the only thing the higher number buys you is an overshoot that costs roughly 10 per cent of your heating energy per degree for as long as you forget about it, around £73 a year per degree at the current gas cap for a typical home.

If rooms heat too slowly, fix the real causes: flow temperature, air in radiators, balancing, throttled TRVs, blocked radiators, and open doors. If the problem is arriving home to a cold house, move the start time earlier or let a smart thermostat's optimum start do it for you. And if you have a heat pump, treat the dial gently, because big jumps can wake an electric backup heater that charges you triple.

Set the temperature you actually want, leave it there, and put your effort into the levers that work.

faq.q: Does the boiler use more gas per minute at a higher thermostat setting?
faq.a: No. On a standard on/off system the boiler fires at the same rate regardless of the setting. A higher number only changes when the boiler switches off, so it runs for longer and uses more gas in total, not more per minute.

faq.q: Does the same apply to electric heaters and air conditioning?
faq.a: Mostly, yes. A plug-in heater with a simple thermostat heats at full power until it reaches the setting, so a higher number does not heat faster. Inverter air conditioning units are the exception, as they can modulate their output, but setting an extreme target still wastes energy once the room passes your real comfort point.

faq.q: Can turning the thermostat right up damage my boiler or heat pump?
faq.a: It will not damage a gas boiler; it just wastes money through overshoot. With a heat pump it can be costly rather than harmful, because a large jump in demand may trigger the backup immersion heater, which produces heat at roughly three times the running cost of the heat pump itself.

faq.q: How long should a room take to warm up from cold?
faq.a: On a typical gas central heating system, expect roughly 30 minutes to an hour to bring a room from cold to comfortable, longer in very cold weather or poorly insulated homes. If it routinely takes much longer, check for air in the radiators, poor balancing, a closed-down radiator valve, or a very low boiler flow temperature.

faq.q: What is the best thermostat setting for a UK home?
faq.a: Most households are comfortable between 18 and 21 degrees, with 18 to 19 fine for bedrooms and hallways and 20 to 21 for living rooms. Every degree lower saves roughly 10 per cent on space heating, which is around £70 a year for a typical gas-heated home at the July to September 2026 price cap.
